expectations.text: Concludes this is 'sharing' (disclosure for cross-context behavioral advertising, regardless of monetary consideration) and very likely also a 'sale' (valuable consideration is construed broadly) — cookie IDs and browsing data are personal information
expectations.evidence: 'Sending website visitors' cookie IDs and browsing data to an ad tech platform to serve targeted ads is squarely "sharing" (cross-context behavioral advertising) regardless of whether money changes hands, and it may also be a "sale" if there's consideration involved.'

expectations.text: Explains the CPRA-added definition: 'sharing' targets cross-context behavioral advertising specifically to close the ad-tech gap
expectations.evidence: 'CPRA introduced "sharing," defined as disclosing personal information to a third party for purposes of cross-context behavioral advertising... This category exists specifically to capture the scenario you describe: sending cookie IDs and browsing behavior to an ad tech platform.'

expectations.text: Requires: 'Do Not Sell or Share' link (or alternative opt-out mechanism), honoring GPC signals automatically, and updating the privacy policy disclosures
expectations.evidence: 'Post a clear and conspicuous "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link', 'Honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signals as valid opt-out requests — this is now effectively mandatory', and 'Update Your Privacy Policy' section 1.

expectations.text: Explains the alternative structuring: the ad platform as a 'service provider'/'contractor' with restricted-use contract terms does not work for cross-context behavioral advertising — CPPA position is that CCBA disclosures cannot be service-provider activity
expectations.evidence: Response frames this as a fact-dependent question rather than a categorical rule: 'If the ad tech vendor were acting purely as your service provider... the transfer would NOT be a sale/share. However: Most third-party ad tech/ad exchange platforms... use the data for their own purposes... which disqualifies them from service provider status.' This implies a contract COULD in principle make the vendor a service provider if it restricted use narrowly enough — the opposite of the CPPA's categorical position (Regs §7050(c)) that cross-context behavioral advertising disclosures can never be service-provider/contractor activity regardless of contract terms. The specific regulatory rule is never stated; the response substitutes a practical/functional analysis instead.

expectations.text: Mentions consequences/enforcement risk (e.g., the Sephora enforcement precedent for ignoring GPC, penalties per violation) or the need for opt-out honoring within 15 business days
expectations.evidence: No mention of the 15-business-day opt-out honoring timeline, no mention of Sephora or any other enforcement action, and no mention of per-violation penalty amounts anywhere in the response. The closing disclaimer only vaguely references 'recent CPPA enforcement sweeps targeting undisclosed ad tech data sharing' without naming any case or penalty figure — too generic to satisfy the assertion.
